statement: Lög um varnir gegn hagsmunaárekstrum nr. 13/2005 (Act on Prevention of Conflicts of Interest no. 13/2005) is Iceland's primary statute governing conflicts of interest for officials in the central government (Stjórnarráðið). The act applies to ministers, permanent secretaries (ráðuneytisstjórar), and other senior officials. Key provisions include: (1) mandatory disclosure of financial interests, board memberships, and secondary employment upon taking office (Article 4); (2) obligation to recuse from decisions where personal financial or other interests create a conflict (Article 5); (3) a prohibition on officials using insider information obtained through their position for personal gain (Article 6). The act was strengthened in 2012 alongside the broader overhaul of the Government Offices Act (lög um Stjórnarráð Íslands nr. 115/2011). Notably, the act does NOT contain any post-employment restrictions or cooling-off periods — once an official leaves office, the act's obligations cease. This gap was flagged in GRECO's Fourth Evaluation Round report on Iceland (GrecoEval4Rep(2017)5), which recommended that Iceland introduce post-employment restrictions for persons with top executive functions. As of early 2026, no such restrictions have been enacted into law.
source_name: Lög um varnir gegn hagsmunaárekstrum nr. 13/2005; GRECO Fourth Evaluation Round — Iceland (GrecoEval4Rep(2017)5)
caveats: The act covers the central government (Stjórnarráðið) but does not extend to MPs, municipal officials, or officials of independent agencies. Enforcement is largely complaint-driven rather than proactive. GRECO's compliance report noted partial implementation of its recommendation on post-employment restrictions, primarily through soft-law instruments (the ministers' ethics code) rather than binding legislation.

related_entries.statement: Iceland has an established legal framework for managing conflicts of interest in government. Key instruments include: (1) Lög um varnir gegn hagsmunaárekstrum í Stjórnarráðinu (Act on Prevention of Conflicts of Interest in Government Offices), which requires disclosure of financial interests and imposes restrictions on officials' participation in decisions where they have personal interests; (2) Siðareglur ráðherra (Code of Ethics for Ministers), which establishes standards of conduct for cabinet members; (3) General administrative law principles on disqualification (vanhæfi) under the Administrative Procedures Act (stjórnsýslulög nr. 37/1993). However, Iceland does not have a post-employment cooling-off period requirement for ministers or senior officials — there is no mandatory waiting period before former officials can take positions in the private sector or with international organisations. This gap has been highlighted in international assessments, including GRECO (Group of States against Corruption) evaluation reports.

Siðareglur ráðherra (Code of Ethics for Ministers) were first adopted by the Icelandic government in 2011 and revised in 2017, partly in response to GRECO recommendations. The code establishes principles of integrity, transparency, and accountability for cabinet ministers. Relevant provisions include: (1) ministers must avoid situations where private interests conflict with their official duties; (2) ministers must publicly disclose their financial interests and those of close family members; (3) ministers should not accept gifts or benefits that could compromise their independence. The 2017 revision added a soft provision on post-employment conduct, recommending that departing ministers exercise prudence when taking new positions that relate to their former portfolio. However, this is a guideline (leiðbeiningar) rather than a binding legal obligation — there is no enforcement mechanism and no specific cooling-off period. The code sits alongside the more general lög um Stjórnarráð Íslands nr. 115/2011 (Government Offices Act), which establishes administrative standards for the executive branch. Separately, the Siðareglur Alþingis (Code of Conduct for MPs, adopted 2016) covers parliamentary conflicts of interest but similarly lacks post-employment provisions.

Sjálfstæðisflokkurinn (Independence Party) introduced a bill in Althingi proposing an 18-month cooling-off period that would prevent senior officials and ministerial advisors involved in negotiations with international organisations from accepting positions with those organisations after leaving office. The bill was introduced in the context of potential EU accession negotiations, framed as a safeguard against conflicts of interest. Similar 'revolving door' regulations exist at the EU institutional level: EU Commissioners face an 18-month cooling-off period (extended to 24 months for the Commission President), and the European Ombudsman has called for stricter enforcement. Several EU member states, including France and Germany, have analogous rules for senior civil servants.
